messages.text: hey team, kicking off billing-v2 this week. the goal is rewriting our billing system to support usage-based pricing — the current flat-rate subscription model isn't cutting it for enterprise customers. I've drafted a pricing model doc that covers the new subscription tiers and how we'll calculate usage per billing cycle. sharing the link in a sec, let's sync Wednesday to go through it

sounds good quinn. I've been looking at how Stripe handles usage-based billing — we can use their metered billing API with usage records. the big question is whether we track usage on our side and sync to Stripe or let Stripe be the source of truth. I'm leaning toward tracking locally in an invoice_items table and syncing aggregate usage at billing cycle end

local tracking + Stripe sync makes sense. we need the granular usage data for the revenue dashboard anyway, and relying on Stripe as the only source makes debugging billing disputes way harder. let's own the data

heads up — billing-v2 is kicking off. we're rewriting the billing system to support usage-based pricing on top of subscription tiers. main pieces: 1) new invoice_items table to track per-customer usage 2) Stripe integration for metered billing and invoicing 3) proration logic for mid-cycle plan changes 4) billing API endpoints for the frontend. @charlie_ross you'll own the API layer, @dave_patel I need you on billing-service infra and deployment pipeline

on it. quick question — are we building the billing API as a separate service or adding endpoints to the existing monolith? separate service would be cleaner for isolation but adds deployment overhead

separate service. billing-service will own all Stripe interactions, invoice generation, and usage aggregation. the existing API gateway will proxy billing routes to it. @dave_patel is setting up the repo and k8s config this week
I'll scaffold billing-service today — Node.js or Go? I'm leaning Go since we'll be processing webhook events at volume and the Stripe webhook handler needs to be fast

Go works. the Stripe Go SDK is solid and we already have Go service templates from the auth work. just make sure we have proper signature verification on the webhook endpoint from day one — I don't want to retrofit that later

quinn, I read through the pricing model doc. for the revenue dashboard, I'll need to track MRR broken down by subscription tier, plus usage revenue on top. should I also build churn rate tracking into this? we don't have that anywhere right now

yes absolutely, churn rate is a must. we need MRR, churn, and expansion revenue (upgrades + usage overage). the board has been asking for these metrics for months. can you mock up the dashboard layout this week so I can get stakeholder buy-in?

billing-service repo is scaffolded. Go service with chi router, structured logging, and Stripe SDK pinned. the Stripe webhook handler endpoint is at /webhooks/stripe with signature verification already wired up. PR #bill-001 is up for review — @alice_chen can you take a look at the project structure?

looks clean. one thing — can you add a dead letter queue for failed webhook events? if billing-service is down when Stripe sends an invoice.paid event, we need to retry processing it. Stripe does retry but I want our own safety net
good idea, I'll add an SQS dead letter queue. failed webhook payloads go there and we process them with a retry worker on a 5min interval

sharing the billing-v2 pricing model for review. we're moving to three subscription tiers: Starter ($29/mo, 1k API calls), Pro ($99/mo, 10k calls), and Enterprise (custom). usage beyond the tier limit is billed at $0.005/call. mid-cycle upgrades are prorated, downgrades take effect at next billing cycle. feedback welcome — @sofia_martinez wanted to see this too for the customer comms plan

one concern with the pricing model — the jump from 1k to 10k calls between Starter and Pro is huge. we might see customers stuck on Starter burning through overage charges instead of upgrading. could we add a mid tier or lower the Pro threshold to 5k?
fair point. let me run the numbers — if we drop Pro to 5k calls at $79/mo it might actually drive more upgrades. I'll update the pricing model doc and reshare

charlie, here's the schema I'm thinking for the invoice_items table: id (uuid), customer_id (FK), subscription_id (FK to Stripe sub), item_type (enum: 'subscription', 'usage', 'proration'), amount_cents (int), quantity (int), period_start, period_end, stripe_invoice_item_id (nullable), created_at. the stripe_invoice_item_id links back to Stripe for reconciliation. thoughts?

looks solid. I'd add a description text field too — for the billing API, the frontend will want a human-readable line item like "API usage: 3,241 calls" on the invoice detail page. also should we add a status column? draft / finalized / voided?
yeah add both. status should default to 'draft' — invoice_items are draft until the billing cycle closes and we finalize the invoice with Stripe. once Stripe confirms, we flip to 'finalized'

migration for the invoice_items table is up in PR #bill-008. adds the core billing data model — tracks subscription charges, usage line items, and proration credits per customer per billing cycle. each row links back to Stripe via stripe_invoice_item_id for reconciliation. @alice_chen approved the schema, requesting a second review from @bob_kim for the indexing strategy

reviewed — the indexes look fine for now. I'd add a composite on (customer_id, period_start, period_end) so the billing API can efficiently pull all line items for a given billing cycle. approved with that suggestion

billing-v2 week 1 update: billing-service is scaffolded (Go), Stripe webhook handler is live with signature verification, invoice_items table migration is merged, and the pricing model has been finalized with product. this week we're building the usage tracking pipeline and starting on the billing API endpoints. on track for mid-October ship

dave, for the usage tracking pipeline — I want billing-service to consume usage events from a Kafka topic, aggregate them per customer per hour, and write to invoice_items. the aggregation avoids creating millions of rows for high-volume customers. can you set up the Kafka consumer and the aggregation worker?

makes sense. I'll batch events into hourly windows and flush to invoice_items with item_type='usage'. for the Kafka consumer — should billing-service manage its own consumer group or share with the existing analytics pipeline?
own consumer group. billing data can't share offsets with analytics — if the analytics consumer falls behind it shouldn't affect billing accuracy

started on the billing API. here's what I'm building: GET /api/billing/invoices (list invoices for a customer), GET /api/billing/invoices/:id (invoice detail with line items), POST /api/billing/subscriptions (create/upgrade subscription), PATCH /api/billing/subscriptions/:id (change plan). the subscription endpoints will talk to Stripe and write to invoice_items for proration credits. sound right?

yep, add GET /api/billing/usage/:customerId too — the frontend needs current-cycle usage for the dashboard widget. pull from invoice_items where period includes the current billing cycle and item_type='usage'

quick heads up — sales wants the ability to apply custom discounts to Enterprise invoices. can the billing API support a discount_cents field on invoice_items? doesn't need to be in v1 but I want to make sure the schema can handle it
we can add it — I'll put a discount_cents nullable column on invoice_items. for v1 it stays null, and we wire it up for Enterprise in a follow-up. low effort to add now and avoids a migration later

alice, for the revenue dashboard — I'm pulling MRR from the invoice_items table by summing all 'subscription' type items for the current period. for usage revenue I sum the 'usage' items. does that logic hold or are there edge cases with proration I should handle?

yes handle proration — when someone upgrades mid-cycle, we create a negative proration credit and a positive charge for the new plan. for MRR, you want to use the subscription amount_cents and ignore proration items. proration is a one-time adjustment, not recurring revenue. I'll add an is_recurring boolean to invoice_items to make this easier to query
that helps a lot, thanks

billing-service usage pipeline is deployed to staging. Kafka consumer is processing usage events, aggregating hourly, and writing to invoice_items. tested with 50k synthetic events — latency is under 200ms per batch. the Stripe webhook handler is also processing invoice.paid and invoice.payment_failed events correctly in staging. @alice_chen ready for your review

nice work. testing it now — one thing I noticed: when Stripe sends a payment_failed webhook, we're logging the error but not updating the invoice status in invoice_items. we need to flip the status to 'failed' so the frontend can show a payment failure banner
good catch, fixing now. I'll also add a retry_count to track how many payment attempts Stripe has made — that way the frontend can show 'payment failed, retrying' vs 'payment failed, action required'

billing API endpoints are live on staging. you can now list invoices, get invoice detail with line items, check current usage, and manage subscriptions. proration is calculated via Stripe's proration preview API and we store the credits in invoice_items. PR #bill-024 — @alice_chen @dave_patel review please

reviewing now. the proration logic looks correct — I like that you're using Stripe's upcoming_invoice preview to show the customer what they'll owe before confirming the upgrade. approved with minor nits on error handling

billing-v2 midpoint check-in: pricing model is locked (Starter $29, Pro $79, Enterprise custom), billing API is on staging, usage pipeline is running, and the revenue dashboard wireframe is with design. we're in good shape for an end-of-October launch. main risk is the Stripe integration testing — we need to run a full billing cycle simulation before go-live

agreed on the Stripe testing risk. I'll set up a Stripe test mode environment this week and simulate a full cycle: subscription creation → usage accumulation → invoice generation → payment → proration on upgrade. @dave_patel can help with automating the test scenarios
revenue dashboard mock is ready — has MRR by tier, usage revenue trend, churn rate over time, and expansion revenue from upgrades. sending the Figma link now. @iris_wang helped me polish the layout

this looks great, exactly what the board needs. love the MRR breakdown by tier. one request — can you add a net revenue retention metric? it's MRR from existing customers including expansion minus churn. that's the number investors care about most
yep, adding it. I can compute NRR from the invoice_items data — just need to compare a cohort's MRR at period start vs end including usage revenue

billing-v2 week 3 update: all core billing API endpoints are on staging, usage pipeline is processing events end-to-end, and Stripe webhook handler covers invoice.paid, payment_failed, and subscription.updated events. this week: full billing cycle simulation in Stripe test mode, proration edge cases, and starting the revenue dashboard backend queries. targeting Oct 14 for staging freeze

dave, I ran the full billing cycle simulation in Stripe test mode. subscription creation and invoicing work perfectly. but there's an issue — when a customer upgrades mid-cycle, the proration credit isn't being written to invoice_items because the subscription.updated webhook fires before we've processed the proration preview. we need to handle the sequence: listen for invoice.updated after the subscription change to catch the proration line items

ah yeah that's a timing issue. I'll add the invoice.updated handler and make it idempotent — if we already have the proration items from the preview API, we update them with the confirmed amounts from Stripe. if not, we create them. fixing today
found another edge case — if a customer downgrades and their usage is already above the new tier's limit for the current cycle, what do we charge? the overage rate of the old tier or the new tier?

good question. @quinn_davis what's the product call here? my suggestion: downgrades take effect at next billing cycle (per the pricing model doc), so current-cycle usage still charges at the old tier's overage rate. usage in the new cycle charges at the new tier's rate
correct — downgrades are deferred to next cycle. the customer keeps their current plan through the end of the billing period. simpler for us and less confusing for customers

fixed the proration webhook timing issue. billing-service now handles invoice.updated events and reconciles proration line items in invoice_items. ran the full upgrade simulation again — subscription → usage → upgrade → proration credit → new invoice, all correctly reflected in the database and confirmed against Stripe. PR #bill-031
beautiful. I verified against Stripe dashboard — numbers match to the cent. merging

kicking off brand-refresh officially this week. our visual identity hasn't been touched in four years and it shows — the logo feels dated, the color palette is inconsistent across surfaces, and we don't have a real typography system. iris is going to lead the visual identity exploration, lisa will handle motion and animation direction, and I'll be reviewing brand direction at each milestone. priya is coordinating the launch timeline with marketing. let's make this one count.

oscar, I've drafted the brand launch timeline — targeting late November for the full rollout including the marketing site, updated brand assets, and the brand book. does that give you enough runway for the design exploration phase? I was thinking 3 weeks for exploration, 2 weeks for iteration, then 3 weeks for implementation.

that's tight but doable. the logo redesign is the critical path — everything else (color palette, typography system, brand book) cascades from the logo direction. if we lock the logo concept by Oct 18 we should be fine. I'll have iris present 3 directions by end of next week.

started moodboarding for the logo redesign. I'm exploring three directions: 1) geometric/minimal — clean wordmark with a custom ligature, 2) abstract mark + wordmark combo — something ownable as an icon, 3) evolution of the current logo with modernized proportions. I'll have rough concepts in the Figma brand file by Friday. any strong preference?

revenue dashboard is functional on staging. pulling live data from invoice_items — MRR by tier, usage revenue, churn rate, expansion revenue, and net revenue retention. all metrics update in real-time as billing events come in. @quinn_davis take a look when you get a chance

I'd lean toward option 2 — we need something that works as a favicon and app icon, not just a wordmark. but explore all three, I want to see them side by side. make sure you test each at small sizes (16px, 32px, 64px) and in both light and dark contexts.
just checked it out — this is exactly what we needed. the NRR chart is going to make the board happy. can you add a date range filter? right now it shows all time but I want to slice by month
easy, adding it today

lisa, as I'm working on the logo directions I'm already thinking about how each one could animate. direction 2 (abstract mark) has the most potential for motion — the mark could have a reveal animation for loading states, page transitions, etc. can you start thinking about motion principles that would work across all three directions? things like easing curves, duration ranges, entrance patterns.

already on it. I'm drafting a motion principles doc — basically the motion chapter of the brand book. thinking: ease-out for entrances (elements arriving), ease-in-out for transitions (state changes), spring physics for interactive feedback. durations between 200-500ms depending on distance traveled. I'll tie the animation curves to the brand personality once we lock the visual direction.

quick update on brand-refresh: we're in the exploration phase this month. @oscar_lee and @iris_wang are developing logo and visual identity directions, and we'll be sharing concepts with the wider team mid-October for feedback. the marketing site refresh will follow once the brand direction is locked. target launch is end of November.

uploaded three logo directions to the Figma brand file — page "Logo Exploration v1". direction A is the geometric wordmark, B is the abstract mark + wordmark, C is the evolved current logo. each one is shown at multiple sizes and on light/dark backgrounds. would love everyone's gut reactions before I present to oscar on Monday.

looked at the Figma brand file — direction B is the strongest by far. the abstract mark has personality and it scales down cleanly. direction A feels too generic, could be any SaaS company. C is too close to what we have now.

from an implementation perspective, direction B is also my favorite. the abstract mark is simple enough to inline as SVG without a performance hit, and it'll work great as a favicon. direction A's custom ligature would be tricky to render consistently across font rendering engines.

we're going with direction B for the logo — the abstract mark + wordmark. iris nailed the exploration. next step: iris will refine the mark, define the color palette (primary, secondary, neutrals, semantic colors), and build out the typography system. the brand book will document all of this. aiming to lock the full visual identity by Oct 25.

starting on the color palette today. proposing a deep navy primary (#0A1628) with a vibrant teal accent (#00C2B2) — it's modern, has great contrast ratios for accessibility, and works on both light and dark backgrounds. I'll build out the full palette with tints, shades, and semantic mappings in the Figma brand file.

billing-v2 staging freeze is today. all billing API endpoints, usage pipeline, Stripe webhook handlers, and proration logic are tested and stable. @charlie_ross @dave_patel @ryan_nguyen — please do a final pass on your PRs. we're cutting the release branch tomorrow for production deploy next week

love the navy + teal direction. make sure we have enough warm neutrals too — pure grays can feel cold. also start thinking about the typography system. we need a heading face, a body face, and a mono face for code blocks. I want something that feels premium but not pretentious.
all my PRs are merged and staging looks good. billing API response times are under 100ms p99. ready for prod
infra is ready. billing-service has health checks, autoscaling configured for webhook spikes, and I've set up PagerDuty alerts for payment_failed webhook errors. deploy pipeline is green

nina, for the typography system I'm looking at Inter for body (we already use it, and it's excellent at small sizes), plus a display face for headings. considering Satoshi or General Sans — both have nice geometric character without being too cold. do you have any strong opinions from a web performance perspective? I want to keep font file sizes reasonable.

Inter is great, zero additional cost since most visitors already have it cached. for the display face, Satoshi is ~120KB for regular + bold which is fine if we subset it. General Sans is heavier. I'd go Satoshi. we can use font-display: swap and preload the woff2 files to avoid layout shift — that'll keep our CLS score clean.

finished the first draft of the hero animation concept. it's the abstract mark assembling itself from geometric fragments — 1.2 second duration, ease-out on the final settle. I think this could be the centerpiece of the marketing site landing page. uploaded the After Effects comp to the Figma brand file as an embedded video.

this is gorgeous. the assembly metaphor is perfect — it tells the story of building something. two notes: 1) can we make the fragments use the new teal accent color before they settle into the final navy? creates a nice color journey. 2) can you also explore a shorter version (400ms) for in-app micro-interactions?
great calls, on both. the teal-to-navy color shift will add depth. I'll do a looping 400ms version too.

billing-v2 is live in production. billing-service is healthy, Stripe webhooks are flowing, and the first real usage events are being processed. monitoring closely for the next 24 hours. huge thanks to @charlie_ross @dave_patel @quinn_davis @ryan_nguyen — this was a clean ship

billing-v2 shipped! we now support usage-based pricing across all subscription tiers, with real-time invoicing, proration on plan changes, and a revenue dashboard with MRR, churn, and NRR metrics. massive effort from the team — @alice_chen @charlie_ross @dave_patel @ryan_nguyen you all crushed it

full visual identity is up in the Figma brand file — color palette (primary, accent, neutrals, semantic), typography system (Satoshi for display, Inter for body, JetBrains Mono for code), logo usage guidelines, and spacing/grid system. please take a look and flag anything before I start compiling the brand book.

24 hours post-launch report: billing-service processed 12k usage events, generated 340 invoices via Stripe, zero webhook failures, p99 latency at 45ms. the dead letter queue is empty. I'd call that a clean launch

the visual identity looks great. I've been working on the tone of voice section for the brand book — our writing should feel confident but approachable, technical but not jargony. I'll send you the draft today. also, marketing wants to know when they can start updating social media templates with the new brand assets.

tell marketing they can start prepping templates after Oct 28 — that's when the brand assets will be final. I want one more round of refinement on the color palette tints before we declare it done. and yes, please send the tone of voice draft, that's an important part of the brand book.

frank, the brand identity is almost locked. I'm going to start scaffolding the marketing site rebuild next week using Next.js. I'll need your help with performance optimization once we start adding the hero animation and image-heavy sections. can you take a look at our current lighthouse score as a baseline? I want to make sure we don't regress.

ran lighthouse on the current marketing site — we're at 72 performance, 85 accessibility. biggest issues are unoptimized images and render-blocking CSS. for the rebuild I can set up next/image with automatic WebP conversion, lazy loading below the fold, and critical CSS inlining. should get us to 90+ pretty easily before we even add the new content.

brand book first draft is done — 34 pages covering logo usage, color palette, typography system, photography style, iconography, motion principles (lisa's section), tone of voice (priya's section), and example applications. uploaded the PDF to the brand-refresh shared drive. let me know what needs revision.

incredible work. read through the whole thing — only substantive feedback is the logo clear space section needs minimum padding specs in both px and percentage terms. everything else is solid. let's finalize this Monday and start distributing brand assets to the team.

brand-refresh update: the visual identity is finalized and the brand book is nearly complete. @nina_scott and @frank_li are starting on the marketing site rebuild this week. we're on track for a late November launch. the new brand assets will be shared with everyone by Monday — please start using them in any external-facing materials.

quinn, now that billing-v2 is stable, I'm writing a post-mortem doc covering the Stripe integration patterns we used — webhook idempotency, proration reconciliation, and the usage aggregation pipeline. it'll be useful for anyone touching billing-service in the future. I'll also document the invoice_items schema decisions for the next engineer who inherits this

starting the marketing site implementation today using the new brand identity. I've set up the Next.js project with the Satoshi + Inter font stack, the full color palette as CSS custom properties, and the responsive grid from the brand book. frank is handling image optimization and performance. if anyone on frontend wants to help with the landing page sections, let me know.

lisa, I need the hero animation exported as a Lottie file so I can embed it on the landing page. the After Effects comp looks amazing but we can't ship a video — Lottie will give us vector quality at a fraction of the file size and it'll be interactive. can you export it by Wednesday?

sure, I'll use the Bodymovin plugin to export to Lottie. one thing — the color transition effect uses some After Effects expressions that don't translate perfectly to Lottie. I might need to simplify the teal-to-navy gradient shift. I'll send you a test export tomorrow so you can check the fidelity.

set up the image pipeline for the marketing site — next/image with sharp for WebP/AVIF generation, blur placeholders for above-the-fold images, and lazy loading for everything below. also added resource hints (preconnect, prefetch) for the font files. current lighthouse score on the empty shell is 98. let's try to stay above 90 as we add content.

98, nice. the hero section is going to be the biggest perf risk — it has the Lottie animation, a large headline in Satoshi, and a full-bleed background. I'm going to lazy-load the Lottie player and use a static SVG as the initial paint so we don't blow up LCP.

nina, I finalized all the brand assets for the marketing site — hero images, feature illustrations, team photos with the new treatment. everything is in the Figma brand file under "Marketing Site Assets" with export specs. let me know if you need anything re-exported at different sizes.

perfect, grabbed them all. the illustrations are beautiful. quick question — the feature section illustrations are at 2x resolution, should I also generate 1x and 3x variants or is 2x enough for the srcset?

2x should be enough for the illustrations since they're vector-based and scale cleanly. for the team photos, 1x and 2x variants would be good — those are raster so they'll look soft on retina if you only serve 1x.

hey team, kicking off platform-api this week. the goal is a public REST API for external integrations plus a developer portal. I've drafted the initial architecture — API gateway with rate limiting, OAuth client credentials for auth, and versioned endpoints. bob I want you leading the database schema and rate limit storage layer, eve you'll own the developer portal frontend, henry you're on the SDK and docs site. let's sync tomorrow morning to walk through the OpenAPI spec draft

marketing wants to review the landing page copy in context before we go live. can you get the landing page to a state where they can see the new brand identity with real copy by Nov 8? doesn't need to be pixel-perfect, just enough to evaluate the marketing copy against the visual design.

yeah, we can have the landing page in a reviewable state by then. hero section is already working with the Lottie animation, just need to drop in the final marketing copy and wire up the feature sections. I'll send you a staging link by Thursday.

sounds good, I've been thinking about the rate limiting storage — redis sorted sets for sliding window counters vs a simple token bucket in postgres. redis is obviously faster but adds an infra dependency. what's your preference? also for the API keys table I'm thinking we store a sha256 hash of the key, never the raw key itself

go with redis for rate limiting — we already have a cluster for session cache so no new infra. good call on hashing the API keys, we should also store a prefix (first 8 chars) in plaintext so users can identify which key they're looking at in the developer portal. @karen_wu designed the API authentication model, loop her in on the key storage scheme before you start the migration

heads up everyone — we're starting the platform-api project this sprint. building a public REST API and developer portal for external integrations. core team is me, @bob_kim, @eve_johnson, and @henry_zhao. if you work on any internal service that might need to be exposed externally, come talk to us. @karen_wu is advising on the API authentication and security model — all auth design decisions go through her

just looked at the developer portal wireframes. are we building this as a standalone Next.js app or embedding it in the main marketing site? also want to confirm — the portal needs to handle API key generation, usage dashboards, and interactive API docs (like a swagger UI equivalent)?

standalone app, separate deploy. yes to all three — key management, usage analytics, and interactive docs powered by our OpenAPI spec. I want developers to be able to try endpoints directly from the portal without needing to install anything. think stripe's API docs as the gold standard

started outlining the SDK structure. thinking we ship a TypeScript SDK first since that covers most of our developer base, then Python. for the docs site should I use the OpenAPI spec as the source of truth and auto-generate the API reference? tools like Redocly or Stoplight can do this pretty cleanly

yes, OpenAPI spec is the single source of truth for everything — the SDK codegen, the API reference, and the interactive docs in the portal. use Redocly for the docs site, it handles versioning well. for the SDK, use openapi-typescript-codegen to scaffold the client and then hand-polish the ergonomics. don't want it to feel auto-generated

Lottie export is done — the hero animation is 47KB which is great. the teal-to-navy color shift had to be simplified slightly but it still reads well. also made a 400ms looping version of just the mark for loading states. both files are in the shared drive.

pushed the initial database schema for platform-api. new tables: api_keys (hashed key, prefix, scopes, rate_limit_tier, created_at, revoked_at), api_requests_log (key_id, endpoint, method, status_code, latency_ms, timestamp), and webhook_subscriptions (key_id, url, events, secret, active). @karen_wu can you review the api_keys table design? specifically the scopes column — I went with a jsonb array but wondering if a separate scopes junction table is cleaner for querying

jsonb array is fine for scopes, we won't need complex scope queries at the db level — scope validation happens in the middleware. more importantly: add a key_version column to api_keys. when we rotate keys we need to support both old and new keys during a grace period. also the webhook_subscriptions table needs an hmac_algorithm column, don't hardcode sha256 — we may need to support sha512 for some enterprise clients
great catches, adding both. will also add a last_used_at timestamp to api_keys so we can flag stale keys in the portal

47KB is perfect. just dropped it into the hero section and it looks incredible — the animation triggers on scroll into viewport and plays once. the loading state version is a nice touch, I'll add that to the page transition component.

just published the first draft of the OpenAPI spec for platform-api v1. covers 14 endpoints across 4 resource groups: /users, /projects, /webhooks, /api-keys. using cursor-based pagination everywhere — no offset pagination. @karen_wu I need your sign-off on the authentication section before we start implementation. the spec uses Bearer tokens from OAuth client credentials flow, API keys passed via X-API-Key header as a fallback

the OpenAPI spec looks solid. one thing — for the developer portal's interactive docs, I need the spec to include example request/response bodies for every endpoint. right now about half of them are missing examples. can you or henry fill those in?

good point, I'll get henry to add examples to all endpoints this week. also want to make sure the portal shows rate limit headers in the response examples — X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset. those are critical for developer onboarding

on it — adding example bodies and rate limit headers to every endpoint in the spec. also noticed we should document the error response format consistently. proposing we use RFC 7807 problem details: {"type", "title", "status", "detail", "instance"}. that way the SDK can have typed error handling
+1 on RFC 7807, that's the right pattern. make sure all 4xx and 5xx responses in the spec use that schema

marketing site rebuild is looking good performance-wise. with all the brand-refresh content loaded (hero animation, illustrations, team photos), we're at 94 lighthouse performance. LCP is 1.8s which is well within the "good" threshold. main CLS issue was the Satoshi font swap causing a layout shift — fixed it by setting explicit font-size-adjust and using a fallback with matched metrics.

staging link is live: staging.bsus.dev/brand-refresh. the landing page has the hero animation, feature sections with the new illustrations, the team section, and the footer with updated brand assets. marketing copy is placeholder in a few spots — can you get the final copy from the marketing team and drop it in the CMS?

rate limit middleware is working. using redis sliding window — each API key gets a counter per minute. default tier is 100 req/min, premium is 1000. the middleware injects X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset headers on every response. returns 429 with Retry-After header when exceeded
nice work. make sure the 429 response body follows the RFC 7807 format henry defined. also add a rate_limit_tier column to the usage dashboard query so eve can show it in the portal

this looks amazing, great work. shared the staging link with marketing, they love it. they'll have final copy by Monday. one piece of feedback: can we make the CTA buttons use the teal accent color instead of navy? they feel like they disappear into the dark hero section.
good catch, swapped the CTAs to teal. much better contrast against the navy hero.

quick update on the developer portal for platform-api — got the basic layout working. API key management page lets you create, rotate, and revoke keys. the interactive API reference is rendering from the OpenAPI spec using a custom Swagger UI theme. next up: usage dashboard with rate limit consumption graphs. @henry_zhao when will the SDK have a downloadable package? want to add an "install SDK" section to the getting started page

SDK is mostly scaffolded — typed clients for all 14 endpoints, automatic pagination helpers, retry logic with exponential backoff. should be publishable to npm by end of next week. I'll send you the package name so you can link it in the portal

the usage API endpoint is ready for the portal dashboard — GET /api-keys/:id/usage returns hourly request counts, error rates, and p95 latency for the last 30 days. data comes from the api_requests_log table, aggregated via a materialized view I set up
perfect, I'll wire that up to the charts this afternoon. can you also expose the current rate limit consumption? I want to show a real-time gauge of how close they are to hitting their limit
yeah I can add a GET /api-keys/:id/rate-limit endpoint that returns current window count vs limit. give me a day

refresh token rotation is implemented in auth-service. when POST /api/auth/token with grant_type=refresh_token comes in: 1) hash the incoming refresh token, 2) look up in session_tokens, 3) if found and not revoked → issue new access + refresh tokens, revoke old refresh token, same token_family. 4) if found but already revoked → REVOKE ALL tokens in that family (theft detection per @karen_wu's guidance).
